title: The Mortgage Rate Conundrum

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This November 2017 Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (Number 829) by Justiniano, Primiceri, and Tambalotti documents a disconnect between mortgage and Treasury interest rates that emerged in summer 2003 following the end of the Fed's expansionary monetary cycle in June 2003. The research, based on millions of loan-level observations, finds that mortgage rates failed to rise according to their historical relationship with Treasury yields, leading to persistently easier mortgage credit conditions.
key_points: Published November 2017 as Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report Number 829
Authors: Alejandro Justiniano, Giorgio E. Primiceri, and Andrea Tambalotti
Documents disconnect between mortgage and Treasury rates in summer 2003, after Fed's expansionary cycle ended in June 2003
Mortgage rates failed to rise according to historical relationship with Treasury yields post-June 2003
Analysis based on millions of loan-level observations controlling for loan, borrower, and geographic characteristics
Delinquency rates rose for loans originated after mid-2003, coinciding with the rate disconnect and cheaper credit
why_it_matters: This research examines a structural anomaly in the relationship between mortgage markets and Treasury yields, showing how monetary policy cycles can produce lasting changes in credit conditions. The findings are relevant to understanding mortgage market pricing mechanisms and the transmission of Fed policy to housing finance.
macro_context: The research examines the period following the end of the Fed's expansionary cycle in June 2003, demonstrating how monetary policy shifts can create disconnects in credit markets. While this is historical analysis, it provides framework for understanding how rate cycles affect mortgage market structure.
market_structure_context: Core finding: mortgage rates became structurally disconnected from Treasury yields following mid-2003, creating persistently easier credit conditions beyond what historical relationships would predict. The loan-level data analysis controls for borrower, loan, and geographic characteristics to isolate this structural shift.
important_numbers: mid-2003 (point when delinquency rates began rising)

human_review.notes: Source is a thin abstract/description only - the full PDF research paper was not captured in the crawl. Historical relevance to mortgage-Treasury relationship but severely limited extractability. Low confidence due to age (2017) and lack of full text access.

notes: Thin source - only abstract metadata was captured. The full research paper is only available in PDF format and was not included in the crawl. This November 2017 Staff Report is a historical research document examining mortgage rate dynamics from 2003-2004. While topic is relevant to market structure (mortgage-Treasury relationship), the lack of full text significantly limits the archival value. Published over 8 years before TODAY. LLM confidence lowered accordingly.
